summary: Martin argues that the software profession urgently needs self-regulation before government intervention becomes inevitable. He illustrates this through a letter from a developer who quit a medical device project after management prioritized schedule over safety, forcing dangerous shortcuts on a surgery device. The developer was made to feel weak for taking a stand while compliant programmers continued building unsafe software. Martin condemns both the criminal manager and the cowardly developers who abdicated their professional responsibility. The post serves as a call to arms for software developers to recognize their ethical obligations given software's pervasive influence on modern life.
key_points: Software touches virtually every aspect of modern civilization yet has no professional regulation or standards
If developers don't self-regulate, government regulation will inevitably follow and 'life will get really bad'
A medical device developer quit rather than ship unsafe software, but was made to feel weak by compliant colleagues
The excuse 'the boss is the boss' represents an unacceptable abdication of professional responsibility
Developers who follow unethical orders are accomplices, not blameless employees
money_quotes: We need to become a self-regulating and self-policing profession. The stakes are simply too high to allow software to remain in the current ad-hoc limbo of hackers, heroics, and hermits.

Any Harry Hacker with a 'J' in his name can get hired to write Java code. And the strongest likelihood is that Harry J. Hacker's code be crap, will be wrong, and will not be explicitly tested before it is shipped.
Plumbers are regulated. Electricians are regulated. Architects, lawyers, and doctors are regulated. Why aren't we?
When I told him we couldn't meet the schedule, he told me I was not allowed to make any more estimates.
The other programmers didn't quit. They told me: 'the boss is the boss – we just do what he tells us – it's his responsibility.'
I am even angrier at the developers. Not only were they accomplices to that criminal idiocy; they made the one guy who took a stand feel stupid and weak.
They are the ones who are stupid and weak. They should not be programmers. Programmers are better than that.
key_insight: Developers who comply with unethical management directives are not absolved of responsibility—they are accomplices who betray the profession's duty to protect the public.
